---
title: "GitHub Commit Patterns – Agent Solution"
output: md_document
---

This notebook solves the commit-message exploration proposed in the project description.  
Key choices:

- Use the balanced sample (`datasets/commits_balanced_3k_per_repo.csv`) to compare repositories fairly.
- **Ignore timezone offsets** when deriving hours/weekday patterns, working with the raw clock times recorded in commits (timezone context is removed).
- Remove obviously auto-generated commits and normalize messages before classifying them.

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.path = "AgentSolution_plots/"
)
options(dplyr.summarise.inform = FALSE)
if (!dir.exists("AgentSolution_plots")) dir.create("AgentSolution_plots")

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(scales)
library(knitr)

source_file <- "datasets/commits_balanced_3k_per_repo.csv"
conv_prefixes <- c("feat", "fix", "docs", "refactor", "test", "chore", "style", "build", "ci", "perf")
# Conventional commit prefixes, matched only at the start of the message
conv_pattern <- str_c("^(", str_c(conv_prefixes, collapse = "|"), ")(\\(|:|\\b)")
weekday_levels <- wday(1:7, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE, week_start = 1)
length_quantile <- 0.98
```

## Load data

```{r load}
raw <- read_csv(
  source_file,
  col_types = cols(
    commit = col_character(),
    author = col_character(),
    date = col_character(),
    message = col_character(),
    repo = col_character()
  )
)
raw_rows <- nrow(raw)
```

Balanced sample with `r raw_rows` commits across `r n_distinct(raw$repo)` repositories.

## Clean and enrich

Steps:

1. Strip timezone offsets from the `date` string to keep the **clock time recorded by the author** (hour-of-day analyses ignore offset shifts; the original timezone cannot be recovered).
2. Parse datetimes; drop missing/invalid and pre-2018 timestamps.
3. Drop duplicate commit hashes.
4. Normalize messages (trim, flatten newlines); remove auto-generated commits that start with *merge*, *bump*, or *revert*.
5. Derive features: `year`, `month`, `weekday`, `hour`, `message_length`, and conventional commit `commit_type` (others → `other`).
   Parsed times keep the recorded clock time and use UTC only as a neutral container after removing offsets (no timezone conversion applied).

```{r clean}
prepared <- raw %>%
  mutate(
    date_stripped = str_replace(date, "\\s[+-]\\d{4}$", ""),
    # Offsets are removed: the POSIXct is stored in UTC but represents the raw clock time from the commit author and cannot be mapped back to their actual timezone
    date_naive = parse_date_time(date_stripped, orders = "a b d H:M:S Y", tz = "UTC", locale = "C"),
    message = str_replace_all(message, "[\r\n]+", " "),
    message = str_trim(message),
    message_lower = str_to_lower(message)
  )

valid_dates <- prepared %>%
  filter(!is.na(date_naive), between(year(date_naive), 2018, 2021))

dedup <- valid_dates %>%
  arrange(date_naive) %>%
  distinct(commit, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Remove obvious automation (merge commits, dependency bumps, and reverts) so the stats reflect human-written messages
auto_pattern <- "^(merge|bump|revert)\\b"
filtered <- dedup %>%
  mutate(auto_generated = str_detect(message_lower, auto_pattern)) %>%
  filter(!auto_generated)

classified <- filtered %>%
  mutate(
    commit_type = str_match(message_lower, conv_pattern)[, 2],
    commit_type = if_else(is.na(commit_type), "other", commit_type),
    commit_type = factor(commit_type, levels = c(conv_prefixes, "other")),
    message_length = nchar(message),
    year = year(date_naive),
    month = as_date(floor_date(date_naive, "month")),
    weekday = factor(wday(date_naive, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE, week_start = 1), levels = weekday_levels),
    hour = hour(date_naive)
  )

clean <- classified %>%
  select(commit, author, repo, date_naive, message, message_length, year, month, weekday, hour, commit_type)
```

### Cleanup summary

Most rows survive once timestamps are parsed without timezone offsets; dropping auto-generated messages keeps the focus on human-written commits.

```{r cleanup-summary}
cleanup_steps <- tribble(
  ~Step, ~Rows,
  "Raw rows", raw_rows,
  "Valid dates >=2018", nrow(valid_dates),
  "Distinct commit hashes", nrow(dedup),
  "After removing auto-generated", nrow(filtered),
  "Ready for analysis", nrow(clean)
)

cleanup_steps %>%
  mutate(Retained = percent(Rows / first(Rows))) %>%
  kable(caption = "Row counts after each cleaning step")
```

### Repository coverage

Top repositories after cleaning (all within 2018–2021 coverage):

```{r repo-span}
repo_span <- clean %>%
  group_by(repo) %>%
  summarise(
    commits = n(),
    first_date = as_date(min(date_naive)),
    last_date = as_date(max(date_naive)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(commits))

kable(head(repo_span, 10), caption = "Top repositories (coverage and span)")
```

## Visualizations

### Overall weekday/hour heatmap

Daily rhythm using recorded local times shows peaks in daytime and a secondary evening wave.
```{r heatmap-overall, fig.width=8, fig.height=4}
heat_df <- clean %>%
  count(weekday, hour)

ggplot(heat_df, aes(hour, weekday, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 23, by = 2)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C") +
  labs(
    title = "Commits by weekday and hour (timezone offsets ignored)",
    x = "Hour of day",
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Commits"
  )
```

### Weekday/hour heatmap by repository (top 6)

Top repositories reveal different rhythms—some concentrated around business hours, others stretching into nights.
```{r heatmap-repo, fig.width=10, fig.height=8}
top_repos <- repo_span %>% slice_head(n = 6) %>% pull(repo)

heat_repo <- clean %>%
  filter(repo %in% top_repos) %>%
  count(repo, weekday, hour)

ggplot(heat_repo, aes(hour, weekday, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 23, by = 4)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "D") +
  facet_wrap(~repo, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Top repos: commits by weekday/hour (local clock preserved)",
    x = "Hour of day",
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Commits"
  )
```

### Commit types per repository

Shares of conventional commit prefixes highlight whether a repo is feature-driven (`feat`) or maintenance-heavy (`fix`, `chore`, `docs`).
```{r types-by-repo, fig.width=9, fig.height=6}
type_share <- clean %>%
  count(repo, commit_type) %>%
  group_by(repo) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(type_share, aes(x = reorder(repo, -pct), y = pct, fill = commit_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", color = "white") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Paired") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Conventional commit prefixes across repositories",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Share of commits",
    fill = "Commit type"
  )
```

### Message length by commit type

Feature and fix commits tend to carry longer descriptions than chores/CI, suggesting richer context for impactful changes.
```{r message-length, fig.width=8, fig.height=5}
ggplot(clean, aes(commit_type, message_length, fill = commit_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.15) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, quantile(clean$message_length, length_quantile)), labels = comma) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel1") +
  labs(
    title = "Message length distribution by commit type",
    x = "Commit type",
    y = "Characters"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

### Monthly activity trend (top 3 repos)

Monthly totals for the busiest repositories show sustained activity with late-2020/early-2021 spikes.
```{r monthly-trend, fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
trend_repos <- repo_span %>% slice_head(n = 3) %>% pull(repo)

monthly <- clean %>%
  filter(repo %in% trend_repos) %>%
  count(month, repo)

ggplot(monthly, aes(month, n, color = repo)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "3 months", date_labels = "%Y-%m") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly commit volume for busiest repositories",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Commits"
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

## Takeaways

- Ignoring timezone offsets (using the recorded local clock) surfaces clear daytime and late-evening peaks; activity spans the full week with softer dips on weekends.
- A handful of repositories dominate volume; their hourly rhythms differ—some cluster around EU work hours, others show broader spread.
- Conventional commit prefixes are widely used; `fix`/`chore` heavy repos contrast with more `feat`-oriented ones.
- Message lengths vary by type: fixes and features are longer than chores/CI, hinting at richer descriptions for impactful changes.
